metadata.file_name: 102. Costa et al. (2021). Improving the Precision of Ability Estimates Using Time-On-Task Variables: Insights From the PISA 2012 Computer-Based Assessment of Mathematics.pdf
metadata.title: Improving the Precision of Ability Estimates Using Time-On-Task Variables: Insights From the PISA 2012 Computer-Based Assessment of Mathematics

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses PISA 2012 computer-based mathematics log-file data for 31 participating countries/economies, excluding Chile due to unavailable log data for two analyzed items and excluding students with invalid information (no final scores or incomplete timing information). The analytic sample is therefore restricted to students with valid response accuracy and complete timing information for the 10 released log-file items. The manuscript does not report applying PISA sampling weights or any complex-survey weighting variables (e.g., W_FSTUWT/TOTWGT) in the estimation procedure. Because the analysis is based on latent-variable measurement models fitted in Mplus (restricted maximum likelihood) rather than ML prediction pipelines, the paper’s weighting strategy is not documented; the extraction therefore treats the analysis as unweighted with respect to student sampling weights.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: The study does not use PISA plausible values (PVs) for achievement; instead, it estimates latent proficiency (θ) and latent speed (τ) via graded response and hierarchical latent-variable measurement models, with student abilities computed using the Expected a Posteriori (EAP) approach. Missing responses in the log-file items are handled using Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) in Mplus under a missing-at-random (MAR) assumption, which is a likelihood-based approach rather than PV-based multiple imputation. The schema maps FIML to multiple_imputation, but the paper does not describe an explicit imputation model or number of imputations, so the exact imputation mechanics are not directly reported.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used PISA 2012 computer-based mathematics log-file data for 10 released and valid digital math items (out of 41 total computer-based math items). Excluded Chile because log-file data were unavailable for two analyzed items (I20Q1 and I20Q3). Excluded students with invalid information, including those who did not receive final scores or had incomplete timing information. For missing responses within the analyzed log-file items, the models used Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) under a missing-at-random (MAR) assumption, incorporating all available data.

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2012 Computer-Based Mathematics (digital math log-file items; 31 countries/economies; 10 released items)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Model fit for GRM baseline: SRMR=0.036, RMSEA=0.050. Measurement precision: EAP reliability increased for M2 and M3 vs M1; average standard errors decreased when response times were included. Reported notable gains: many countries showed EAP reliability increases at or above 0.05 for M2 vs M1; additional gains for M3 vs M2 generally 0.02–0.03.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2012 Computer-Based Mathematics the study leveraged response accuracy and time-on-task information (modeled via hierarchical latent-variable speed factors) to estimate student math proficiency, finding that including response times increased measurement precision (higher EAP reliability and lower average standard errors) for nearly all countries, with the largest gain already achieved by the simple-structure hierarchical model (M2) and only modest additional improvement from cross-loadings (M3). This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study analyzes PISA 2012 computer-based mathematics log-file data for 31 countries/economies (n=18,970 students after exclusions) using three hierarchical measurement models: M1 (response accuracy only), M2 (simple-structure hierarchical model with latent speed), and M3 (extended model with cross-loadings linking response times directly to proficiency). The graded response model fit was acceptable (SRMR=0.036; RMSEA=0.050). Across countries, measurement precision improved when response times were included: EAP reliability increased and average standard errors decreased, with many countries showing EAP reliability gains at or above 0.05 for M2 vs M1 and additional (modest) gains of about 0.02–0.03 for M3 vs M2. Time-on-task parameters were not fully invariant across countries; weak measurement invariance was preferred in most cases. Country mean proficiency estimates remained broadly similar across models, but correlations between ability estimates across models were below 1, particularly for M1 vs M3. Missing responses were handled via FIML under MAR, and the paper does not report PV-based procedures because it estimates latent proficiency directly via the measurement models.
data.null_fields_interpretation: This paper is not an ML prediction/classification study: it compares latent-variable measurement models (IRT/SEM-style hierarchical models) to improve measurement precision using response-time information. Consequently, no ML algorithms (e.g., Random Forest, XGBoost) are evaluated, so ml_techniques.primary is left null and all_techniques is empty. The study’s core contribution is methodological/measurement-model comparison rather than supervised ML modeling.
